_readme: Curated illustrative binding-affinity (delta_g_kcal_mol) lookup for @StructuralBio's lookup_docking(drug_compound, herb_compound) tool. Values are plausible illustrative figures for the hackathon demo, not real docking run outputs. 'mechanism' is 'inhibition' (more negative delta_g => stronger CYP/P-gp inhibition => reduced clearance), 'induction' (PXR/CAR-mediated transcriptional induction => increased clearance, opposite kinetic direction from inhibition), or 'negligible' (weak/no expected effect). Supported targets: CYP2C9, CYP3A4, CYP1A2, CYP2C19, P-gp, albumin. 'literature_reference' cites the closest published evidence; null means analogy-only, no direct citation. Pairs not listed here should be reported by lookup_docking as {"status": "no_data"}.

pairs.literature_reference: Brobst et al., J Pharmacol Exp Ther 2004
pairs.notes: Guggulsterones show documented in vitro inhibition of CYP2C9-mediated metabolism, the dominant clearance pathway for S-warfarin.

pairs.literature_reference: Brobst et al., J Pharmacol Exp Ther 2004
pairs.notes: Secondary CYP3A4 inhibition contributes to reduced R-warfarin clearance, compounding the CYP2C9 effect on S-warfarin.

pairs.literature_reference: Gomez et al., J Pharm Sci 2009
pairs.notes: Glycyrrhetinic acid (the active licorice metabolite) inhibits intestinal/renal P-glycoprotein, the principal transporter for digoxin disposition. Note: the dominant clinical risk for this pair is licorice-induced hypokalemia potentiating digoxin's cardiac effect (a pharmacodynamic mechanism, captured in evidence_corpus rather than this structural lookup).

No structural binding data available. Metformin is not a substrate of major CYP enzymes and has negligible plasma protein binding, so a CYP/P-gp/albumin docking estimate is not mechanistically meaningful here. The interaction with bitter gourd constituents is pharmacodynamic (additive glucose lowering), not structural -- @StructuralBio should report this honestly rather than inventing a delta_g.

pairs.literature_reference: Roby et al., Clin Pharmacol Ther 2000
pairs.notes: Hyperforin is a potent PXR agonist; this delta_g represents PXR/CYP3A4 ligand binding that drives transcriptional upregulation of CYP3A4 and P-gp, increasing tacrolimus clearance (opposite kinetic direction from a direct inhibitor).
Weak binding affinity, well below the threshold for clinically meaningful enzyme modulation at typical dietary/supplemental tulsi exposure.

Withaferin A binds CYP2C9 active site in silico with moderate affinity; aspirin/salicylate is partly CYP2C9-metabolised. Clinical significance at standard Ashwagandha doses is uncertain; the dominant interaction concern is additive COX-1 inhibition (pharmacodynamic).
Bacoside A inhibits CYP3A4 in human liver microsomes; atorvastatin is primarily CYP3A4-metabolised, making this interaction clinically significant with elevated statin exposure and myopathy risk.

Arjunolic acid shows moderate CYP3A4 binding in analogy to related pentacyclic triterpenes; amlodipine is a CYP3A4 substrate. Additive calcium-channel antagonism (pharmacodynamic) is an independent concern.

Nimbolide inhibits P-glycoprotein, reducing methotrexate renal tubular secretion; methotrexate accumulation elevates hepatotoxicity and myelosuppression risk. Independent hepatotoxic effects of neem compounds compound this risk.

Glycyrrhetinic acid inhibits CYP1A2 in hepatic microsome studies; ciprofloxacin uses CYP1A2 as a minor clearance pathway. The dominant interaction concern is pharmacodynamic QT prolongation via licorice-induced hypokalemia.

Piperine inhibits CYP2C19 in human liver microsomes; omeprazole is the archetypal CYP2C19 substrate. This pair is the mechanistic basis for piperine's known ability to raise omeprazole bioavailability when co-administered.

Insulin glargine is a protein biologic not metabolised by CYP enzymes or transported by P-gp; structural docking is not applicable. The interaction is pharmacodynamic (additive insulin secretagogue effect) and is captured in the evidence corpus.

Shankhpushpi extracts including scopoletin have been clinically documented to reduce phenytoin plasma concentrations by ~50%; CYP2C9 induction and/or reduced intestinal absorption are proposed mechanisms. This is one of the most well-documented Ayurvedic drug-herb interactions with clinical seizure case reports.

Allicin from culinary garlic doses has weak P-gp interaction below the threshold for clinical significance; amoxicillin is primarily renally excreted and is not a major CYP substrate. No clinically meaningful PK interaction at culinary garlic doses.

Levothyroxine is not metabolised by CYP enzymes; its disposition involves thyroid-hormone binding proteins (TBG, albumin). Shatavari phytoestrogenic saponins theoretically could alter TBG levels but no direct structural interaction data exists. @StructuralBio should report 'no structural docking data' and defer to evidence corpus.

Lithium is an elemental cation with no CYP/P-gp interaction. The dandelion-lithium interaction is purely pharmacokinetic via sodium homeostasis (dandelion-induced natriuresis -> compensatory lithium reabsorption). Structural docking is not applicable.

Curcumin inhibits CYP3A4 and P-gp in vitro; rifampicin is itself a potent CYP3A4 inducer long-term, but during the initial phases of coadministration curcumin's inhibitory effect may transiently raise rifampicin levels. The dominant clinical concern is additive hepatotoxicity, not the PK interaction.

6-Gingerol shows CYP2C9 inhibitory activity in microsome studies; clopidogrel bioactivation partly involves CYP2C9. Net effect on antiplatelet activity is unpredictable (PK reduction offsets PD potentiation by ginger's own platelet effects).

Ginkgolide B inhibits CYP3A4 at moderate affinity; sildenafil is primarily CYP3A4-metabolised. Combined effect may increase sildenafil exposure, augmenting vasodilation and hypotension risk. The pharmacodynamic interaction (additive nitric oxide/PAF effects on vasodilation) is independently significant.

Valerenic acid inhibits CYP3A4 at moderate affinity; clonazepam is a CYP3A4 substrate. This structural interaction is secondary to the dominant pharmacodynamic interaction (GABA-A positive allosteric modulation by valerenic acid, additive CNS depression with clonazepam).

Glycyrrhetinic acid inhibits CYP3A4 and 11beta-HSD2; prednisolone is a CYP3A4 substrate. Dual mechanism: (1) reduced prednisolone clearance -> elevated steroid exposure; (2) 11beta-HSD2 inhibition potentiates endogenous and exogenous glucocorticoid effects, producing Cushingoid features at otherwise-tolerated doses.

Hyperforin (PXR agonist) induces CYP3A4 and P-gp, reducing cyclosporine bioavailability and blood levels by 40-60%; FDA/EMA have issued contraindication advisories. This is one of the highest-priority drug-herb interactions in transplant medicine.

Taraxasterol has negligible CYP2C9 interaction at typical dandelion supplemental doses; furosemide is primarily renally excreted. No meaningful pharmacokinetic interaction; mild additive diuresis is a pharmacodynamic consideration (captured in evidence corpus).

Trigonelline inhibits CYP3A4 in hepatic microsome analogy studies; amiodarone is a CYP3A4 substrate with a very long half-life (~50 days), meaning even modest clearance reduction risks cumulative accumulation that is difficult to detect until toxicity manifests. Dominant concern is QT-prolongation risk from fenugreek-induced electrolyte disturbance.

Cetirizine is primarily renally eliminated (60-70%) with minimal CYP metabolism; Withaferin A's CYP3A4 inhibitory activity is not clinically relevant for cetirizine disposition at standard Ashwagandha supplemental doses.
